text: A seventy-four-year-old woman who underwent per-oral endoscopic myotomy for achalasia presented three months later with resolved dysphagia but increasing reflux refractory to PPI therapy. Upper GI demonstrated the presence of moderate spontaneous gastroesophageal reflux above the level of the aortic arch. Partial fundoplication was recommended to the patient.

The surgeon uses an optical five millimeter port to enter the insufflated abdomen, placing two additional five millimeter and two ten millimeter ports in an M configuration in the upper abdomen. A flexible retractor elevates the left lateral segment of the liver, revealing no adhesions or scarring from the prior myotomy. An upper endoscopy shows the gastroesophageal junction wide open without signs of esophagitis.

The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to open the gastrohepatic and phrenoesophageal ligaments in two layers, preserving the hepatic branch of the vagus nerve. The peritoneum along the right and left crura is incised, and the esophagus is circumferentially mobilized.

The surgeon performs minimal dissection along the anterior surface of the esophagus, with no visible scarring from the prior myotomy. The lesser sac is accessed along the greater curvature of the stomach, inferior to the spleen's lower pole. The short gastric vessels are divided using a harmonic scalpel up to the level of the left crura, ensuring complete mobilization of the posterior aspect of the stomach fundus.

The surgeon identifies both anterior and posterior vagus nerves and encircles the esophagus with a vessel loop. The crural dissection is completed, allowing the posterior aspect of the upper fundus of the stomach to be passed behind the distal esophagus. A Toupee fundoplication is constructed using three rows of interrupted 2-0 silk sutures. Superior sutures are placed at the ten o'clock and two o'clock positions between the esophagus, stomach fundus, and diaphragm, with two additional sutures on either side between the esophagus and stomach fundus.

The surgeon secures the posterior aspect of the fundus to the crura using two 2-0 silk sutures. The vessel loop is removed, and the fundoplication is completed without tension. An upper endoscopy shows no obstruction at the gastroesophageal junction, with a newly formed posterior antireflux valve visible around the lower esophageal sphincter.
